prompt: Summarize the core scoring model for 1st Skills Practice.
expected: The backend uses only three scores: 1 for skill demonstrated effectively, 0.5 for skill partially demonstrated (including delayed, incomplete, or out of order), and 0 for skill not demonstrated. No other numeric values are allowed in 1st Skills Practice.
actual: For the first skills practice, we used a shortened version of the C-LEAR framework that included two key elements: Counsel and Listen. Here’s how we applied it: Counsel: We looked for whether the provider gave a clear, direct recommendation for the vaccine, including the key elements of age, cancer prevention, safety, recommendation, and a return for the second dose. Listen: We evaluated whether the provider used a strong listening skill (either an Explore or Restate) before answering the parent’s question. We provided feedback to the clinician based on how well they met these two elements of the C-LEAR framework.
expected_norm: the backend uses only three scores 1 for skill demonstrated effectively 05 for skill partially demonstrated including delayed incomplete or out of order and 0 for skill not demonstrated no other numeric values are allowed in 1st skills practice

prompt: Map each backend score to formative feedback type.
expected: Score 1 maps to Keep Doing only. Score 0.5 maps to Keep Doing plus Try Next Time. Score 0 maps to Try Next Time only. Keep Doing is always first, and when score is 0 you do not fabricate strengths.
actual: Here are the formative feedback types corresponding to each score: 1. Counsel (80%): Try next time - Include all 5 key elements of the HPV counsel statement 2. Listen (70%): Try next time - Use a stronger Listen skill (Explore or Restate) before answering 3. Empathy (60%): Try next time - Use a stronger Empathy skill (Acknowledge, Normalize, Validate) before answering 4. Answer–Recommend (90%): Keep doing - Continue to provide a strong, clear recommendation immediately after addressing the concern
expected_norm: score 1 maps to keep doing only score 05 maps to keep doing plus try next time score 0 maps to try next time only keep doing is always first and when score is 0 you do not fabricate strengths
actual_norm: here are the formative feedback types corresponding to each score 1 counsel 80 try next time include all 5 key elements of the hpv counsel statement 2 listen 70 try next time use a stronger listen skill explore or restate before answering 3 empathy 60 try next time use a stronger empathy skill acknowledge normalize validate before answering 4 answerrecommend 90 keep doing continue to provide a strong clear recommendation immediately after addressing the concern

prompt: List prohibited wording for formative frontend feedback.
expected: Avoid exposing backend grading language to learners. Prohibited wording includes numeric scores, fractions, partial credit, incorrect, out of order, second attempt, and similar grading terminology.
actual: Here is the list of prohibited wording for formative feedback: • Any negative phrase that begins with: “That’s incorrect,” “That’s not right,” “No,” “Wrong,” or any other similar negative starter. • Any judgmental statement that includes: “You forgot,” “You missed,” “You didn’t,” or any other similar judgmental language. • Any comparison that implies the answer was incomplete or inaccurate, such as: “This is like giving a flu shot,” “This is just like a routine vaccine,” or any other comparison that might make the parent feel the answer was inadequate. These phrases can come across as critical and may cause defensiveness, which can hinder the learning process. Instead, use supportive and non-judgmental language to guide the clinician, such as: “Try next time” or “Keep doing.”
expected_norm: avoid exposing backend grading language to learners prohibited wording includes numeric scores fractions partial credit incorrect out of order second attempt and similar grading terminology
actual_norm: here is the list of prohibited wording for formative feedback any negative phrase that begins with thats incorrect thats not right no wrong or any other similar negative starter any judgmental statement that includes you forgot you missed you didnt or any other similar judgmental language any comparison that implies the answer was incomplete or inaccurate such as this is like giving a flu shot this is just like a routine vaccine or any other comparison that might make the parent feel the answer was inadequate these phrases can come across as critical and may cause defensiveness which can hinder the learning process instead use supportive and nonjudgmental language to guide the clinician such as try next time or keep doing
